question: How is the Dirichlet (or Pigeonhole) principle formally stated?
answer:
The **Dirichlet principle** (also called the **Pigeonhole principle**) is formally stated as:

> If \(n+1\) objects are placed into \(n\) boxes, then at least one box contains **at least 2** objects.

More generally:

> If \(N\) objects are distributed among \(k\) boxes, then at least one box contains at least  
> \[
> \left\lceil \frac{N}{k} \right\rceil
> \]
> objects.

A common equivalent form is:

> If more than \(n\) objects are assigned to \(n\) categories, then some category must contain at least two objects.

This is a standard combinatorial principle. The retrieved course excerpt did not include the formal statement directly, so I provided the formal statement from the standard definition.

A = set of all integers from 1 to 1000 that are multiples of 3 ➔ $|A| = ?$

From 1 to 1000, every third integer is a multiple of 3, each multiple of 3 is of the form $3p$, for any integer $p$ from 1 through $[1000/3]=333$, Hence $|A| = 333$.

B = set of all integers from 1 to 1000 that are multiples of 5 ➔ $|B| = ?$

Similarly for multiples of 5, each multiple of 5 is of the form $5q$ for some integer $q$ from 1 through $[1000/5]=200$. Hence, we have $|B| = 200$.

A ∩ B: set of all integers from 1 to 1000 that are multiples of 15 ➔ $|A \cap B| = ?$

To determine the number of multiples of 15 from 1 through 1000, each multiple of 15 is of the form $15r$ for some integer $r$ from 1 through $[1000/15]=66$. ➔ $|A \cap B| = 66$.
